text: So this is an eighteen year old with an elevated white count and a CT which was concerning for appendicitis with an eight millimeter appendix. So I took him for robotic appendectomy.
The surgeon performs a mini diagnostic laparoscopy to visually inspect the appendix, which appears normal, and to rule out the presence of a Meckel's diverticulum.
The surgeon creates a defect in the meso-appendix using a bipolar device, targeting it for ligation. The cauterized segment is then cut using a mega suture cut tool.
Using just two instruments offsets the cost to about a direct cost of just four hundred dollars.
The surgeon performs a suture ligation at the base of the appendix using a 2-0 silk suture, wrapping it around to achieve a double ligation.
The surgeon uses the mega suture cut to excise the tissue, then delivers it out through the port or places it in a 5mm anocatch bag if the tissue is too large for the port.
The surgeon performs a Z-plasty imbrication on the appendiceal stump, using a slip knot technique to ensure proper tension and correct imbrication of the tissue.

This is really good practice for the residents, as I already am assured that the appendix is sealed off with the usage or ligation. This is just extra. So I form an air knot. And then I convert that air knot to a slip knot by pulling up on the needle side and down on the side that went out of the tissue last. And then create my adequate tension for the invocation. And then I can keep the tension on the needle side and convert it back to a square knot. And then tie it up.
